interrogator_dossier:
FICO (Fair Isaac Corporation) — a deep dive on management and the setup
Prepared from eight quarters of earnings-call transcripts, from the quarter ended July 31, 2024, through the quarter ended April 28, 2026. Signal: deep value, on enterprise value relative to gross profit.

1. HOW THE STORY HAS CHANGED

Across these eight quarters the FICO story went through a regime change, not in the business, which performed flawlessly throughout, but in the external structure around the crown-jewel asset (the FICO score's conforming-mortgage monopoly).

Phase one (mid-2024 into early 2025): a pricing-cycle compounder, monopoly intact, with a regulatory tailwind pending. The early calls were a victory lap built on two engines. First, the mortgage-score pricing cycle: the wholesale royalty was raised to $4.95 per score for calendar 2025 (quarter ended November 6, 2024), with mortgage-origination revenue up 80% (quarter ended July 2024), 95% (quarter ended November 2024), and 110% (quarter ended January 2025), almost entirely on price. Second, the FICO Score 10-T conforming-adoption story: the pending move by the housing-finance regulator to replace Classic FICO with 10-T for government-backed loans. Management paraded a growing 10-T signup roster ($126 billion, then $244 billion, $261 billion, and $284 billion in annualized originations across these calls). Software and Applications was always the quieter sibling: low-single-digit total growth that masked a 30%-plus platform engine offset by a shrinking legacy book.

The first crack (call dated February 4, 2025, for the quarter ended January 2025): chief executive Lansing disclosed, almost in passing, that "In January, the FHFA announced it no longer has a specific timeline for the implementation" of 10-T for conforming mortgages. The load-bearing catalyst slipped from "imminent requirement" to "indefinite." Management's tone stayed calm, but the clock on the bull case had been reset.

Phase two (mid-2025 to present): a defensive re-architecting around a competitive threat. By the quarter ended November 5, 2025, the narrative pivoted hard. The new centerpiece was the FICO Mortgage Direct License Program, selling the score directly to tri-merge resellers (Xactus, Cotality, Ascend, CIC, MeridianLink), explicitly "eliminating reliance on the 3 nationwide credit bureaus." This was framed as pro-competition and affordability ("aligning with calls from policymakers... to modernize credit infrastructure"), but it is unmistakably a pre-emptive defense: FICO restructuring its own distribution and floating a new performance-pricing model ($4.95 plus a $33 funding fee) ahead of a known competitive event. The 10-T signup roster kept climbing ($313 billion, then $377 billion, then $495 billion in serviceable originations), but it quietly became a moat-defense talking point rather than a catalyst talking point.

The turning point (quarter ended April 28, 2026): management confirmed the housing-finance and housing regulators moved to admit VantageScore 4.0 into the conforming market, and FICO responded by slashing its 10-T performance pricing from "$4.95 per score plus $33 funding fee" to "$0.99 per score plus $65 funding fee." The monopoly-premium story was over; the new story is "we still win on volume even in a two-score world, and the mortgage pricing-and-volume cycle is so hot it swamps the share noise."

Dropped versus added: quietly dropped, the "the regulator will force 10-T adoption" thesis (dead since January 2025). Quietly added, VantageScore as a named rival, trigger-lead volume risk, a potential 10% credit-card-rate cap as a wild card, and an aggressive artificial-intelligence platform narrative (FICO's FFM, FLM, and FSM foundation models) that did not exist in 2024.

2. CLAIMS VERSUS THE FINANCIALS

The defining feature of FICO is that the financials have, if anything, over-delivered against the narrative; the gap is not story-versus-numbers, it is numbers-versus-stock-price.

- Claim of "strong, accelerating growth": revenue rose from $1.32 billion in fiscal 2021 to $1.99 billion in fiscal 2025 (up 15.9% year over year, a 13.1% three-year compound annual growth rate), and in the quarter ended April 28, 2026, revenue was up 39%, Scores up 60%, and mortgage-origination revenue up 127%. This is richer than the narrative: the pricing cycle compounded faster than any guidance implied.
- Claim of "record free cash flow... returning capital and then some": free cash flow was up 26.7% year over year, trailing-twelve-month free cash flow reached $893 million (versus the scan's $607 million annual figure), and the company bought back $605 million of stock in the quarter ended April 28, 2026, alone, at an average of $1,251. Fully corroborated: this is a genuine cash machine buying its own falling stock heavily.
- Claim of "best-in-class, expanding margins": gross margin 82.2%, net margin 32.7%, free-cash-flow margin 38.7%, adjusted operating margin 58% (quarter ended April 2025), and a 41.0% return on invested capital. Corroborated: among the best unit economics in all of software and data.
- Claim of "we're a screaming value even at a price-to-earnings above 100" (quarter ended November 2024): the stock has since fallen from a 52-week high of $1,998 to $1,137 (about -43%) and now trades at roughly 36 times free cash flow, cheaper than Verisk (about 45 times) and MSCI (about 55 times). The buyback call was wrong on timing (repurchases in fiscal 2024 averaged $1,366 and in the quarter ended January 2026 ran at $1,707) but right on the long-run cash thesis; the current $1,251 purchases look far better.
- Claim of "no share loss in any vertical... volume, not share, is what matters" (quarter ended April 28, 2026): this is an unverifiable forward statement. VantageScore 4.0 is now live at the top-30 originators including Rocket, and FICO cut 10-T price 80% ($4.95 to $0.99); the action contradicts the reassurance. The narrative is poorer than the action: you do not cut price 80% on a product whose share you are unworried about.
- The reported return on equity of -110.6% is an accounting artifact of negative book equity from years of buybacks funded partly with debt (net debt of $2.8 to $3.44 billion), not operational distress, with an Altman-Z score of 10.5 and a Piotroski score of 8 of 9. This is a misleading metric: return on equity is meaningless here, while the 41% return on invested capital is the true return signal.

Where the story is poorer than the trajectory: the Software and Applications segment. Management leans on "37%/54% platform revenue growth" (first and second quarters of fiscal 2026) and "$350M platform ARR," but total segment revenue grew only 2% (first quarter of fiscal 2026) and 7% (second quarter) because the non-platform legacy book is in structural decline of 12% to 13%. The segment is a smaller, slower-growth asset than the prepared remarks imply, which matters for valuing the parts separately.

3. TONE AND CONFIDENCE OVER TIME

The trajectory is steady-to-rising on the business, but subtly defensive on the regulatory and competitive overlay, and that split is the single most important tell.

- On the fundamentals, rising, almost serene. From the quarter ended April 2025: "record-breaking free cash flow... the largest single quarter buyback in FICO history." From the quarter ended April 28, 2026: "a very strong quarter and a great start... we are increasing our fiscal 2026 guidance." No hedging on the core engine.
- On the regulatory and competitive overlay, measured, deflective, and increasingly "your guess is as good as ours." When the timeline died (quarter ended January 2025): "there's no telling what a new administration might do... one just doesn't know." On VantageScore gaming risk (quarter ended April 28, 2026): "of course, we don't know how this is going to unfold... it's almost inevitable... in a 2-score system." On the credit-card rate cap: "Your guess is as good as mine." This is a chief executive who has stopped trying to control the regulatory narrative and shifted to reassuring that it will not hurt volume.
- From offensive to mixed. Capital-allocation language stayed aggressively offensive throughout, with buybacks the constant. But the go-to-market language flipped from "we have a small but dynamic direct salesforce" (passive, 2024) to "we've been fairly aggressive this year in expanding that headcount" (quarter ended January 2026), a real, dated shift from harvest mode to invest mode that pressures near-term margins.

The long-running joke, "every single call it seems like our stock is at an all-time high... why are you still buying back?" (quarter ended November 2024), reads very differently now that the stock has halved. Management has not repeated it since the decline.

4. HOW RELIABLE IS GUIDANCE

Reliability is high; this is the most credible name-and-management pairing in the set on delivery.

- Documented over-delivery: "Exceeded fiscal '24 guidance on all metrics" (quarter ended November 2024); "exceeded fiscal '25 guidance on all metrics and delivered record annual free cash flow" (quarter ended November 2025). The scan corroborates a beat in 6 of 7 quarters (86%).
- Explicit, admitted conservatism. Finance chief Steve Weber, repeatedly and on the record: "we build a model that we believe is what's likely to happen, and then we will take that and haircut that expectation... we want to be able to exceed" (quarter ended November 2025); "this year, probably more than other years where we're extra conservative." They told you the guide is deliberately low. The guidance raise in the quarter ended April 28, 2026, plus "you'd probably still think we were being too conservative" (quarter ended January 2026) is the tell.
- Reconciliation: consensus forward earnings-per-share growth is 25.8%; trailing-twelve-month diluted earnings per share of $31.57 versus fiscal 2025 reported $26.54 shows the run-rate is already well ahead of the trailing year, consistent with a deliberately conservative guidance machine.

The one caveat: guidance is reliable on what management controls (price, cost, buyback) and explicitly abdicated on what it does not (mortgage volume, which "varies mostly with interest rates... your guess is as good as ours"). The beat streak is real but rests on a conservatively-set volume floor in a rate-sensitive end market.

5. WHERE ANALYSTS PUSHED HARDEST

Analysts circled three topics relentlessly, and management's answers grew thinner on the one that matters.

1. The regulatory and VantageScore competitive threat (every call, escalating). Simon Clinch (Redburn) pushed repeatedly on the odds of regulatory implementation; by the quarter ended April 28, 2026, the questions were pointed, with Owen Lau on adverse selection and gaming and Sean Kennedy (Mizuho) on lenders pulling both scores. Management's fallback, "it is technically share loss for FICO, but it's not volume loss... you're really expanding the market by the second pull," is the load-bearing reassurance of the entire bull case, and it is unverified and self-serving. This is the topic analysts pushed on that did turn into bad news (the 80% price cut).
2. Software and Applications deceleration and the shape of new bookings. Wurtzel (Wolfe) and others repeatedly probed why total software growth is low-single-digit and whether bookings strength (trailing-twelve-month annual contract value of $126 million, up 36%) is a budget flush or durable. Management consistently denied any pull-forward and pointed to a new sales leader and platform momentum, credible but not yet visible in segment revenue.
3. Cost creep in the Scores segment. Craig Huber (Huber Research) repeatedly flagged Scores operating expense up roughly 39% year over year (quarter ended April 2025). Management attributed it to consumer cost of goods plus deliberate marketing and headcount investment, meaning margin is being spent forward, a real near-term drag analysts correctly identified.

What analysts worry about that management is not truly addressing: the long-run sustainability of the mortgage-score cash cow's pricing and volume once a price-competitive rival is blessed by the enterprises. Management's answer is always "share is not the same as volume"; it never quantifies the pricing umbrella it is now visibly lowering.

6. WARNING SIGNS AND POSITIVE SIGNS

Warning signs:
- The conforming-monopoly advantage has been structurally breached. The housing-finance and housing regulators admitted VantageScore 4.0 into Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac underwriting (live April 2026; top-30 originators on board). This is the single most important fact and it is permanent: the days of a sole-source government-backed credit-score monopoly are over (quarter ended April 28, 2026).
- An 80% price cut on the flagship growth product: 10-T performance pricing went from $4.95 to $0.99 per score (quarter ended April 28, 2026). Whatever the volume-offset framing, this is a defensive markdown that caps the per-score ceiling the bull case was built on.
- Insider behavior contradicts the bull narrative. The accurate count is zero open-market purchases against four sales (2,825 shares); the grant-inflated 1.05 buy ratio in the scan is noise, and the real signal is that insiders did not step in despite a 43% decline.
- Negative book equity and $3.44 billion of net debt from debt-funded buybacks: fine while free cash flow compounds, but it removes the balance-sheet cushion and makes return on equity uninterpretable.

Positive signs:
- The cash engine is accelerating, not decaying. Quarter ended April 28, 2026: revenue up 39%, Scores up 60%, mortgage-origination revenue up 127%, adjusted earnings per share up 60%, trailing-twelve-month free cash flow of $893 million, and full-year 2026 guidance raised to about $2.45 billion. A "deep value" tag on a business growing the top line 39% is itself the anomaly.
- An aggressive, now well-timed buyback: $605 million in the quarter ended April 28, 2026, at an average of $1,251 (versus $1,707 a quarter earlier), with chief executive Lansing saying "I would certainly consider our stock at these levels to be an opportunistic time." The share count is shrinking into the decline.
- Demonstrated pricing power and a conservative-guidance track record (an 86% beat rate, self-admitted haircuts): the floor under estimates is real.
- Optionality the market is ignoring: the Direct License Program bypassing the bureaus (margin and control upside), 10-T buy-now-pay-later and UltraFICO and Plaid cash-flow scores (expanding the addressable market), and a credible artificial-intelligence platform pivot.

7. SUBTLER SIGNALS

- The language migrated from "mandate" to "optionality." In 2024, 10-T was framed as something the regulator would require; by fiscal 2026 every reference is to "optionality to the market," "two alternative pricing models," "performance pricing." The shift from compulsion to choice is the linguistic fingerprint of a monopoly becoming a competitor.
- The funding-fee reframing. Watch the pricing mechanics: $4.95 plus $33 became $0.99 plus $65. FICO is de-emphasizing the visible, attackable per-score price that VantageScore undercuts and loading monetization into a per-funded-loan funding fee, capturing previously-unmonetized downstream usage (ratings agencies, mortgage-bond investors, insurers, regulators, per Lansing's list in the quarter ended November 2025). This is a sophisticated re-pricing of the whole value chain, not a simple discount, and it is under-appreciated.
- Capital-allocation discipline is unchanged, but management's self-perception shifted. The "screaming value at 100 times earnings" bravado (quarter ended November 2024) has gone silent; the language is now the more sober "we tend not to be market timers, although we have leaned in much more heavily on an opportunistic basis" (quarter ended April 28, 2026), a chief executive recalibrating his own valuation confidence after a halving.
- New voices: a new software sales leader (cited in the quarters ended November 2025 and January 2026) credited with the bookings acceleration, and a wholly new artificial-intelligence vocabulary (the FFM, FLM, and FSM foundation models, 137 AI patents) defending against the worry that AI commoditizes scoring.
- Competition discussed defensively for the first time. For years management dismissed competition ("not a lot of competitive threat, not a lot of new innovation from competitors," quarter ended November 2024). By the quarter ended April 28, 2026, they are explicitly war-gaming VantageScore adverse selection and Upstart and Consumer Financial Protection Bureau precedents, a tonal admission that the moat is contested.

8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION TAKEAWAY (for the portfolio decision)

Classification: a stable business with a competitive structure that is turning. The operating company is strengthening (accelerating revenue, record free cash flow, expanding margins, disciplined buybacks). But the investment case is turning from "unassailable sole-source monopoly compounding at a premium multiple" to "a dominant-but-now-contested data franchise re-rating to a competitive multiple while harvesting one final, enormous pricing-and-volume cycle." Both are true at once, which is exactly why the stock halved while earnings exploded.

The single most important thing to weigh before sizing: the load-bearing catalyst has already fired, and it fired against FICO. The housing-finance regulator's decision (the entire reason this was ever a 10-T catalyst story) resolved in April 2026 by admitting a price-competitive rival into the conforming market rather than mandating FICO's premium product. The re-rating is therefore spent, not pending; what remains is a valuation and expectations question, not an event question. The decision is whether roughly $1,137 (about 36 times free cash flow, below lower-return peers Verisk and MSCI) already discounts a permanently lower monopoly premium more than enough to offset a business compounding free cash flow at 25% to 30% with admittedly conservative guidance, that is, whether the market is pricing terminal monopoly erosion into a still-accelerating cash machine. This is a deep-value, re-rate-the-multiple thesis, not a mispriced special situation. Size it as a quality compounder on sale, not as an event trade, and respect that the one hard data point on conviction (insider open-market activity) is zero purchases and four sales.

Management and setup credibility: solid (4 out of 5). Trajectory: turning.
